summary: Gruber publishes his annual Apple report card for 2022, awarding high marks across most categories. The Mac earns an A driven by the M2 MacBook Air and Apple silicon's dominance, while iPhone also gets an A despite being an incremental year. iPad receives a B, dragged down by stale Pro hardware designs and a conceptually muddled Stage Manager. Apple Watch and Wearables both earn A's, with the Ultra and AirPods Pro 2 standing out. Services and Apple TV get B's, HomeKit a C, and Developer Relations a troubling C due to ongoing App Store resentment.
key_points: The M2 MacBook Air is praised as 'the best laptop for most people ever made,' and Gruber argues Apple silicon is decisively beating x86 competition with no ARM competitors for serious PCs.
Gruber controversially predicts Apple is more likely to stop selling iMacs entirely than to add a larger model, arguing the Studio Display plus replaceable Mac Mini/Studio is the superior approach.

Stage Manager on iPad feels half-baked, and Gruber confesses he feels more productive on his small iPhone than his large iPad — calling iPadOS conceptually muddled compared to the clarity of the early iPad era.
Developer relations remains a sore point: Apple's goal should be making developers want to create software exclusively for Apple platforms, but the opposite is happening due to App Store policies.
The AirPods Pro 2 are called 'the best single expression of Apple as a company today' — simple, useful, and offering features most people want.
money_quotes: Apple silicon is absolutely pantsing its x86 competition, and no one else is making ARM chips for serious PCs.
I think it's more likely that Apple will stop selling any iMacs at all than add a larger model.
The Dynamic Island is a wonderfully inventive design: useful, attractive, fun.
I suppose it's actually untrue but I'll be damned if it doesn't feel like I'm more productive on my small-screen iPhone than I am on my large-screen iPad. It's the damnedest thing.
The new AirPods Pro are the best single expression of Apple as a company today.
Apple's goal should be for developer relations to be so good that developers want to create software exclusively for Apple's platforms. The opposite is happening.
Apple Music has a great catalog but a lousy confusing software interface — quite possibly Apple's worst app.
It's starting to feel downright miserly, though, that Apple is still offering only a mere 5 GB of storage at the free tier.
key_insight: Apple's 2022 was defined by hardware excellence — especially Apple silicon and AirPods Pro — but software vision on iPad and developer relations remain conspicuous weak spots that incremental polish cannot fix.
